cards.title: Parquet — The Primary Format
cards.teaser: Parquet is the file format DuckDB is most optimized for — columnar, compressed, and metadata-rich enough to enable predicate pushdown and column pruning.

runs.text: Parquet is the default choice for DuckDB workloads. The format's columnar layout matches DuckDB's internal execution model, and its rich metadata enables significant query optimization.

explanation.code:
# Write to Parquet
con.execute("COPY orders TO 'orders.parquet' (FORMAT PARQUET)")

# Or use PyArrow integration
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
arrow_table = con.execute("SELECT * FROM orders").fetch_arrow_table()
pq.write_table(arrow_table, 'orders.parquet', compression='zstd')

items.text: Column pruning:
only reads columns referenced in the query.
Predicate pushdown:
filters using min/max statistics in row group metadata to skip row groups.
Dictionary encoding:
for low-cardinality columns, avoids reading repeated string values.

runs.text: Use ZSTD compression for Parquet files that will be queried repeatedly. Compared to Snappy (Parquet default), ZSTD achieves 20-40% better compression with similar or faster decompression speed.
Like a columnar spreadsheet that the database can read selectively — instead of reading the whole file, DuckDB reads only the columns and row groups it needs.

cards.title: CSV — Auto-Detection and Manual Override
cards.teaser: DuckDB's CSV reader auto-detects delimiters, headers, and types in most cases — but knowing when to override matters for production reliability.

explanation.code: COPY (SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 1000) TO 'sample.csv' (FORMAT CSV, HEADER true);

-- Query all CSVs in a directory
SELECT * FROM read_csv('logs/*.csv', union_by_name=true);

runs.text: aligns columns by name rather than position — useful when multiple CSV files have slightly different column orders.
Auto-detected types for date columns default to VARCHAR if the format is ambiguous (e.g.,
— month/day or day/month?). Always specify explicit types for date columns in production pipelines.
with better defaults — it guesses right most of the time, but production code should always specify the schema explicitly.

cards.title: JSON — Reading Nested Data
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads JSON files and JSON columns natively, including nested and semi-structured data, with functions to extract, navigate, and flatten the structure.

runs.text: DuckDB reads JSON files directly, handling both newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) and JSON arrays.

explanation.code:
-- Unnest a JSON array field into rows
SELECT
    event_id,
    unnest(json_extract(payload, '$.tags')::VARCHAR[]) AS tag
FROM events;

COPY orders TO 'orders.json' (FORMAT JSON);
COPY orders TO 'orders.ndjson' (FORMAT JSON, ARRAY false);

runs.text: For very large JSON files with deeply nested structures, consider converting to Parquet first:
. Subsequent queries on the Parquet file will be significantly faster.
with a full SQL query optimizer — navigate nested JSON structures with paths, but aggregate and join across millions of records at the same time.

cards.title: Apache Iceberg Support
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads Apache Iceberg tables directly in 2026 — including snapshot time travel and partition pruning — making it a capable Iceberg query engine without Spark.

runs.text: extension provides native Iceberg table reads. As of DuckDB 1.1, Iceberg support is bundled in the distribution (no separate install needed in most contexts).

items.text: Reading Parquet-backed Iceberg tables (v1 and v2 format).
Partition pruning on Iceberg partition specs.
Snapshot time travel.
Schema evolution awareness (reads with current schema mapped to historical snapshots).
Integration with AWS Glue Data Catalog via the
aws
extension.

runs.text: What DuckDB does not support (as of 2026):

items.text: Writing to Iceberg tables (reads only).
Sort order optimization hints from Iceberg metadata.
Full REST catalog integration for all catalog implementations.

runs.text: For write-path Iceberg workflows, pair DuckDB with a dedicated Iceberg writer (Spark, Flink, or an Iceberg REST catalog client).
DuckDB + Iceberg on S3 is a compelling alternative to Athena for ad-hoc Iceberg queries — lower latency for interactive queries, no per-scan cost, and zero infrastructure beyond the DuckDB binary.
Like reading a versioned document store — the table tracks its own history, and DuckDB can open any version like checking out a git commit.

cards.title: Delta Lake Support
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads Delta Lake tables via the delta extension — including time travel and change data feed — for teams already invested in the Delta ecosystem.

runs.text: extension adds Delta Lake read support. It became a community extension in DuckDB 1.1.

items.text: Reading Delta v1 and v2 tables.
Time travel by version number and timestamp.
Partition pruning.
Schema evolution (reads latest schema by default).
Deletion vectors (DV-aware reads so soft-deleted rows are excluded).

runs.text: Delta vs Iceberg for DuckDB users in 2026:

items.text: Both are read-only in DuckDB. For writes, use a Delta-native writer (Spark, Delta-rs, polars).
Delta extension is community-maintained vs Iceberg which ships with DuckDB core.
Choose based on your existing ecosystem — Delta if your warehouse is Databricks, Iceberg if it is AWS Glue or Snowflake.

explanation.code:
import duckdb
con = duckdb.connect()
con.install_extension('delta')
con.load_extension('delta')
result = con.execute("SELECT * FROM delta_scan('s3://bucket/table/')").df()

runs.text: Delta Lake's transaction log is a directory of JSON files. DuckDB reads the log to determine which Parquet files constitute the current table state, then reads those files directly. The query plan bypasses the Delta engine entirely.
Like reading a git repo's history to figure out which files are in the current checkout — the log tells you what exists, then you read the actual files.

cards.title: Excel Support
cards.teaser: DuckDB's Excel extension reads .xlsx files directly as tables — useful for the significant amount of real-world data that lives in spreadsheets.

runs.text: extension adds read and write support for

explanation.code:
COPY (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE year = 2024)
TO 'output.xlsx' (FORMAT XLSX);

con.install_extension('excel')
con.load_extension('excel')
df = con.execute("SELECT * FROM read_xlsx('data.xlsx')").df()

runs.text: Practical use: ETL for stakeholder data that arrives as Excel files. Instead of converting to CSV first, query the Excel file directly and transform in DuckDB.

items.text: Only
.xlsx
format (Open XML). Older
.xls
binary format is not supported.
No formula evaluation — cell values only, not formula results. Excel must have calculated the values before DuckDB reads them.
Images, charts, and formatting are ignored.

runs.text: Combine Excel reading with Parquet writing for a simple "spreadsheet to data lake" pipeline:
Like treating a spreadsheet as a database table — skip the export-to-CSV step and query the Excel file directly as if it were already structured data.

cards.title: Arrow IPC — Zero-Copy Interop
cards.teaser: DuckDB's Arrow integration enables zero-copy data exchange with any Arrow-compatible library — no serialization overhead when moving data between DuckDB and Pandas, Polars, or cuDF.

runs.text: Apache Arrow defines a columnar in-memory format and IPC (Inter-Process Communication) serialization format. DuckDB uses Arrow as its primary interoperability layer.
The key property: DuckDB's internal columnar format is Arrow-compatible. Transferring a result set to an Arrow table requires no data copying — DuckDB hands over a pointer to its internal buffers.

explanation.code:
import duckdb
import pyarrow as pa

con = duckdb.connect()

# Zero-copy result to Arrow table
arrow_table = con.execute("SELECT * FROM 'data.parquet'").fetch_arrow_table()

# Pass Arrow table back to DuckDB for further querying
result = con.execute("SELECT region, SUM(revenue) FROM arrow_table GROUP BY region")

runs.text: Arrow streaming (for large results that don't fit in memory):

explanation.code:
# Fetch as a streaming RecordBatch reader
reader = con.execute("SELECT * FROM large_table").fetch_record_batch(rows_per_batch=50000)
for batch in reader:
    # Process 50,000 rows at a time
    process(batch)

import polars as pl

# Query DuckDB, get Polars DataFrame
df = con.execute("SELECT * FROM 'data.parquet'").pl()

# Pass Polars DataFrame to DuckDB
polars_df = pl.read_parquet('data.parquet')
result = con.execute("SELECT * FROM polars_df WHERE value > 100").df()

-- Read Arrow IPC stream file
SELECT * FROM read_arrow('data.arrows');

runs.text: "Zero-copy" means DuckDB does not copy the data when handing it to Arrow. The Arrow table references DuckDB's memory. If the DuckDB connection is closed before the Arrow table is consumed, the referenced memory is freed. Always consume Arrow results before closing the connection.
Like passing a reference rather than a copy — the data stays in one place in memory; both DuckDB and the Arrow consumer just hold a pointer to it.

cards.title: Hive Partitioning
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads Hive-partitioned directory structures and propagates partition keys as virtual columns — enabling efficient partition pruning across large datasets.

runs.text: Hive partitioning organizes data files into directories named
, creating a physical partition structure. DuckDB understands this layout and uses it for query optimization.

explanation.code:
data/
├── year=2023/
│   ├── month=01/
│   │   └── events.parquet
│   └── month=02/
│       └── events.parquet
└── year=2024/
    ├── month=01/
    │   └── events.parquet
    └── month=02/
        └── events.parquet

COPY orders TO 'output/' (FORMAT PARQUET, PARTITION_BY (year, month));
-- Creates output/year=2024/month=01/data.parquet, etc.

runs.text: clause filters on a Hive partition key, DuckDB skips reading the non-matching directories entirely. For a dataset with 48 monthly partitions, a query filtered to one month reads approximately 1/48 of the data.
Write Hive-partitioned Parquet with the columns you filter on most frequently as the outer partition keys. Date-based partitions (
) are the most common choice for event and log data.
Like file system folders as database indexes — the directory structure encodes the partition key values, and skipping a folder skips all the data in it.

cards.title: DuckLake — A DuckDB-Native Table Format
cards.teaser: DuckLake is an open table format designed around DuckDB's strengths — simpler than Iceberg with a SQL-managed catalog stored in a DuckDB database.

runs.text: DuckLake is an open table format introduced in 2025, designed specifically for DuckDB-centric architectures. It addresses the operational complexity of Iceberg and Delta by storing the catalog metadata in a DuckDB database file rather than a directory of JSON files.

items.text: Catalog storage:
DuckLake stores metadata in a DuckDB file (SQL tables). Iceberg stores metadata as JSON files in object storage alongside the data.
Transactional catalog:
DuckLake catalog operations use DuckDB's ACID transactions. This eliminates the eventual-consistency concerns of Iceberg's JSON-file-based catalog.
Simpler tooling:
read and write DuckLake with DuckDB directly, without a separate catalog server.

runs.text: DuckLake in 2026: growing adoption for DuckDB-native pipelines where teams want lake format benefits (ACID writes, time travel, schema evolution) without Iceberg's operational overhead. Less ecosystem tooling than Iceberg — not yet readable by Spark or Flink natively.

DuckLake is the right choice when DuckDB is your primary query engine and you do not need to share the table format with Spark, Flink, or other systems. For multi-engine environments, Iceberg remains the more portable choice.
Like SQLite for the catalog layer — instead of a complex distributed metadata store, the catalog is just a database file you can open and inspect.

cards.title: Format Performance Comparison
cards.teaser: The file format you choose affects query speed by an order of magnitude — columnar compressed formats like Parquet dominate for analytical workloads, but each format has tradeoffs.

runs.text: Format performance for analytical queries on a 10GB dataset (representative benchmark, not universal):

items.text: Storage: ~2GB (5:1 compression typical)
Read speed: fastest for column-selective queries; DuckDB reads only needed columns
Predicate pushdown: yes (row group statistics)
Best for: all new analytical datasets
Storage: ~3-4GB
Read speed: slower than Parquet — row-oriented, requires parsing every column even for selective queries
Predicate pushdown: none
Best for: interoperability and source files from systems that don't produce Parquet
Storage: ~5-8GB uncompressed
Read speed: slowest — each line requires JSON parsing; no column pruning
Best for: API responses, log files, event streams
Storage: Parquet storage plus small metadata overhead
Read speed: comparable to raw Parquet plus partition pruning via table metadata
Best for: when you need ACID writes, time travel, or schema evolution
Storage: uncompressed, approximately equal to in-memory size
Read speed: fastest for programmatic access — zero deserialization overhead when reading into Arrow-compatible tools
Best for: inter-process data handoff, not long-term storage

runs.text: For a dataset you will query many times, the one-time cost of converting CSV or JSON to Parquet pays back in seconds on the first query and every query after. For a 10GB CSV file, the conversion takes under a minute; subsequent query times drop from minutes to seconds.
Like choosing between JSON and binary serialization — the choice that is easiest to write is rarely the fastest to read, especially when you need it millions of times.
